words: SAM SCHRAGER:  Did your folks come right to Arrow Junction too? Did you know each
other
from then?
EDWARD GROSECLOSE: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: What were they figuring on when they came out: were they going to farm or

speaker: SS
words: what?

speaker: DG
words: Well, I don't know, your folks just came West because they were looking for

speaker: DG
words: more land, wasn't it Ed? Or what?

speaker: EG
words: Yes, there was homestead land in this country then.

speaker: DG
words: Most all of 'em homesteaded.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder how they happened to pick Arrow Junction?

speaker: EG
words: Well, now, it wasn't Arrow Junction then, it was Potlatch. Potlatch Junction.

speaker: EG
words: And to tell what caused them to be that way; my oldest brothers

speaker: EG
words: come to this country alookin' fer good cattle country, and they landed up

speaker: EG
words: here at Oaksdale. And they went to searchin' the country from there down

speaker: EG
words: and they come in .down here in about 1900, the older brothers. And they

speaker: EG
words: went down the top of that hill there in January and there was two feet of

speaker: EG
words: snow up there, and they dropped down in the canyon and there wasn't no snow

speaker: EG
words: in there at all. And it was one of these years 4"hat it seemed to be that

speaker: EG
words: we had good early rain in the fall and the grass was great, big, clear to

speaker: EG
words: the top of their shoes down in there and wild horses, fat aid nice. Home

speaker: EG
words: stead land, they figured that was the place they was lookin' for. Well,

speaker: EG
words: you know, there's been about ten years since that that they's been good

speaker: EG
words: grass here in the fall. (Chuckles) And the rest of the time it's been

speaker: EG
words: pretty dry. Lots of times it didn't amount to any grass at all. Well, it'd

speaker: EG
words: ? up, you know, before anything grows. But this, right here now, is one

speaker: EG
words: spring that's holdin' things back. I believe it's one of the worst that

speaker: EG
words: we've had for being this fur in March, you know nothing has started to grow.

speaker: EG
words: The winter's been nice.

speaker: SS
words: Were your brothers out here then before your father was?

speaker: DG
words: Yes, they was- Yeah, had three brothers here that had homesteads when we

speaker: DG
words: come out here.

speaker: SS
words: They told them about the—? Where did they take up their homesteads around

speaker: SS
words: here?

speaker: DG
words: Well, my brother, Will, took up a homestead in Catholic Gulch, where

speaker: DG
words: Margaret Smith lives. That was his homestead there. Smiths bought more

speaker: DG
words: land than what he had around there, but then he had 160, where Margaret lives

speaker: DG
words: where her house is, that's where his was. Then my oldest brother, his home

speaker: DG
words: stead was where that pile of junk is over there back of Slickapoo's. It's

speaker: DG
words: about three and a half miles below town here. Gene Groseclose, his young

speaker: DG
words: est son has got part of the place there now. And he's got a house partly

speaker: DG
words: built over there on it. Well, Gene's got forty acres of the 160.

speaker: SS
words: So there was still homestead to be taken around here when they came out.

speaker: EG
words: Yes, at that time.

speaker: DG
words: Still wild horses runnin' on the range when they came here.

speaker: EG
words: Well, the Grosecloses kind of spoilt that. The horses run on the top of

speaker: EG
words: that Potlatch Rdjige up there, and my brother, Albert, his homestead is down

speaker: EG
words: there where that great, big walnut tree is above the road, about six miles

speaker: EG
words: below here. There's a great, big walnut stand right above the road. Well

speaker: EG
words: he planted that there in 1903. That's when that tree was planted there.

speaker: EG
words: And my brother, Marion, he come West with us a.id my sister. They was old

speaker: EG
words: enough to take claims. My brother went and looked at a place up on top of

speaker: EG
words: the Potlatch Ridge. Before he got around to homestead it, why, Old Tommy

speaker: EG
words: Summers took it. So, then, he took a place a mile long down there and he

speaker: EG
words: got that nice bench that's over across the Potlatch; he got that.

speaker: DG
words: You mean the Albrights?

speaker: EG
words: No, Albrights hasn't, that's what what Wilkes got over across, and then he

speaker: EG
words: had about sixty acres on this side of the river. It's down there where they

speaker: EG
words: got them trailer houses now set up above the road; that is where his homes

speaker: EG
words: stead house was, was right there.

speaker: SS
words: Then he came out with you and your parents?

speaker: EG
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: This would have been what?

speaker: SS
words: I'm surprised that there was still homesteading to be had around here, I'm

speaker: SS
words: really surprised.

speaker: EG
words: Then my sister she got a place where  Albrights got down there.

speaker: EG
words: They was a little piece of ground showed up there— well, the road at that

speaker: EG
words: time went right below that old house that's down there, went right below

speaker: EG
words: that and. around that hill; around that bluff, all the way around there.

speaker: DG
words: You know where the Albright place is?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, I do.

speaker: HG
words: Well, where the Albrights house is down there, that was on a place that

speaker: HG
words: Floyd Leach got. He took out homestead all that bluff up there, but, where

speaker: HG
words: her house is his, but it's just a few rods below , my sister's place

speaker: HG
words: went to there.

speaker: SS
words: So what about you? You were still a kid and lived with your folks?

speaker: EG
words: I was just a kid, I was too young for that.

speaker: SS
words: Where did your folks move? Where did they take? Did they take a home

speaker: SS
words: stead?

speaker: EG
words: Well, my dad he thought he would get a homestead down there where that bluff

speaker: EG
words: is across the river? All that long bluff from the Little Potlatch up there?

speaker: EG
words: Well, the Indians turned in and put their fences over this^way, so it made

speaker: EG
words: look like they was a lot of land over there next to^Potlatch. So, he

speaker: EG
words: took a claim in there, but when they surveyed it out, why, there was scar

speaker: EG
words: cely anything but the river and that bluff, so he turned it loose again.

speaker: SS
words: Was this all land that got opened up after they opened up the reservation?

speaker: EG
words: That was the reservation. Then he homesteaded again up in the hills, back

speaker: EG
words: up in the timber country, they got a wrong location for the corner, you know,

speaker: EG
words: and they thought they found a good piece of ground , and he homesteaded,

speaker: EG
words: and when they surveyed it out, it was another bluff as bad as that there, so

speaker: EG
words: he turned it loose again. He never kep' it. So he never

speaker: DG
words: He bought a place on Fix Ridge.

speaker: EG
words: He bought a place up on the Fix Ridge, here, 120 acres, and kept that for

speaker: EG
words: quite a number of years.

speaker: SS
words: You mean the Indians had turned it so that it didn't look like it was—

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, they just run their fences on farther up the hill than what they was

speaker: EG
words: supposed to. And from where their fences was up against the hill, well the

speaker: EG
words: length of their place left a lot of land over there next to the Potlatch.

speaker: EG
words: But when they come and surveyed it out their place went close to the river

speaker: EG
words: so there wasn't nothing in there worth anything.

speaker: SS
words: Well, where he bought on Fix Ridge, was that mostly cleared?

speaker: EG
words: That was farm land.

speaker: SS
words: Had that land on Fix Ridge, had that all been bunch grass to start with?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, yes, long years ago that was.

speaker: SS
words: Did he have much of that that he was growing a crop on?

speaker: EG
words: That was all broke out long before he got toe ;hc£.

speaker: DG
words: The thing that really interested the brothers when they come, the first one,

speaker: DG
words: was they could have had land on top of the hill, that was farm land more—

speaker: EG
words: No, they couldn't. That was took up, they'd a had to bought it then on top.

speaker: DG
words: They saw the good and there was apples, hanging on the

speaker: DG
words: trees that weren't frozen and green grass, tall, and everything, so they

speaker: DG
words: thought that they'd come to the Garden of Eden, so they all settled down in

speaker: DG
words: the canyon.

speaker: EG
words: Well, now then, they're havin' a lot of arguments now that makes me awful

speaker: EG
words: mad about protectin' these wild animals.

speaker: DG
words: Maybe he doesn't want that. tie wants something else.

speaker: SS
words: Let's talk about the early days more.

speaker: EG
words: That'll lead back to the early days. When Old Man Fix, he was the first man

speaker: EG
words: that come on the Fix Ridge up here, the prairie chickens, oh, they was by

speaker: EG
words: the thousands up there, and he went out there and made him a boxtrap with

speaker: EG
words: a trip board, and he put a sheaf of grain up there and the birds'd come out

speaker: EG
words: to get that on the end of that board, you know, and they'd drop 'em in the

speaker: EG
words: box. They come a little snow and Sam Tabor, stepson of he was along

speaker: EG
words: about fourteen at that time, he went out to see about the trap one morning

speaker: EG
words: come arunnin' back, he says, "Run out here quick,the birds are flyin'

speaker: EG
words: away with that trap!" It just git so full, you know, til they was just about

speaker: EG
words: to carry it off. So that's the way the thing was when the white man

speaker: EG
words: come here.

speaker: SS
words: What happened to all those prairie chickens?

speaker: EG
words: Well, that's it! The white men killed out an awful lot of 'em, and then—

speaker: EG
words: well they was a peculiar bird, when you scared 'em up, they'd just fly over

speaker: EG
words: the next hill and light, just out  Well, a man could foiler a

speaker: EG
words: bunch of 'em all day, if he wanted to. They wouldn't fly over a ha If a

speaker: EG
words: mile, you know, just over the top of one draw, on top of the other one,

speaker: EG
words: you could follow 'em. Well, there wasn't so very many up there— oh, quite

speaker: EG
words: a lot, too, on the Ridge when we went up there in 1904. But then the squir

speaker: EG
words: rels got so bad that the farmers put out poison. And that cleaned the Prair

speaker: EG
words: ie chickens up entirely.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yeah. It was the poison, not so much the hunting that did them in?

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, they was an awful lot of em done in before they finished them up by

speaker: EG
words: poison.

speaker: SS
words: I think somebody has said something like that to me. I'm interested that

speaker: SS
words: you say that too.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah. Well, the first man-

speaker: DG
words: Them days, as well as now.

speaker: EG
words: - one of the oldest men in the country located over there on the Genesee,

speaker: EG
words: was Old Man Gibb. And you see these trails around these hills here on the

speaker: EG
words: rocks? We call them cayuse trails when we come here, but Gibb called 'em

speaker: EG
words: deer trails. Yeah, he says them was deer trails. And people when we come

speaker: EG
words: here said there wasn't no buzzards in this country. Gibb was the first man

speaker: EG
words: here and he said there was. But the white men, the first ones that come

speaker: EG
words: here in order to get rid of the coyotes, they poisoned dead horses and

speaker: EG
words: that's what a buzzard will eat, you know. Well, they killed all the buz

speaker: EG
words: zards. But the coyotes they got pretty wise, they finally got wise til

speaker: EG
words: they wouldn't eat poisoned horse. They learnt better'n that. And the magpiece's

speaker: EG
words: another thing that's awful hard to poison. There were lots of 'em

speaker: EG
words: here and we learnt how to poison 'em. When you butcher, get the blood,

speaker: EG
words: considerable bucket of blood and let it clot, and then put strychnine in it

speaker: EG
words: and hang it up on a post on the fence, and them magpies'11 rush to that,

speaker: EG
words: but don't let'em stay there. Don't let 'em stay there five minutes, get

speaker: EG
words: down there and chase 'em away, and they'll keep comin' back. If you let

speaker: EG
words: one git sick there, he'll get sick— imitates magpie cry- you'll never git

speaker: EG
words: another magpie. They ucmerstand what they're sayin', and you'll never git

speaker: EG
words: another magpie if one ever gits sick where your poison is. But if you keep

speaker: EG
words: 'em scared away, why you can just keep takin' 'em.

speaker: SS
words: They drink it right out of the bucket?

speaker: EG
words: Yes, that clotted blood, you know, they just eat that. But they was here,

speaker: EG
words: oh, they was here by the thousands when we come here.

speaker: SS
words: Did they do much damage?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, did they! They would steal the eggs out from under a settin' hen, and

speaker: EG
words: they'd steal the little chickens, and they was just a terrible bird. And

speaker: EG
words: in order to get rid of 'em, they went every spring out and burnt all the

speaker: EG
words: nests you could find all over the country. Just about the time— just before

speaker: EG
words: they hatched; burn 'em all up. And shoot any that close enough, but

speaker: EG
words: they're wise about that gun business. They give you plenty of room when

speaker: EG
words: they know you've got a gun out.

speaker: SS
words: Did all this cut down the size of the population very much? Did they cut

speaker: SS
words: the numbers?

speaker: EG
words: The magpies?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah.

speaker: EG
words: Oh, yes, there's very few in the country now.

speaker: SS
words: Compared to what there was then.

speaker: EG
words: Well, I've seen 'em light .on a big thorn bush and they'd be enough on it,

speaker: EG
words: that they'd make it bend. They'd be a thousand magpies on a thorn bush.

speaker: EG
words: No^ then, I don't suppose you could find twenty of em in a bunch anywhere

speaker: EG
words: in the country. There's scarcely any.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when you came into this canyon, was the kih^ of trees and the way it

speaker: SS
words: was growing on the land, was it pretty much the way it is now? Or was it

speaker: SS
words: very different?

speaker: EG
words: the same as it is now. There wasn't much timber. Very little.

speaker: EG
words: Little more along the Potlatch. That Potlatch has quit the cross there, it

speaker: EG
words: used to run right over by the railroad. And the railroad come in there and

speaker: EG
words: you see that made it stay on the other side of the railroad. Sometimes in

speaker: EG
words: a big flood it would come clear over it, spread all over that flat down

speaker: EG
words: through there. But when the railroad made it stay over there that started

speaker: EG
words: a cuttin', so instead of being over next to the railroad, now it's over against

speaker: EG
words: the other hill. It just worked that flat down there over.

speaker: EG
words: Well, right down here below town, where §herman's barn is? There was a good

speaker: EG
words: nice pear orchard on the other side of the river there when we come here.

speaker: EG
words: Quite a bunch of trees in there in that flat. The Potlatch just kep movin'

speaker: EG
words: over and took 'em all out and went clear over against the hill. Made 'em

speaker: EG
words: move the houses over there over further. They finally built a house way up

speaker: EG
words: on the hill and then they sold it out, there's nobody over there at all any

speaker: EG
words: more.

speaker: DG
words: Well, there were quite a lot of orchards here.

speaker: SS
words: Were there a lot of orchards here right then?

speaker: EG
words: Yes, there was. There was more orchards.

speaker: DG
words: More in later years, too.

speaker: EG
words: Down here where Bill Cartan lives, that flat in there at his place was a

speaker: EG
words: big apple orchard. Large trees then, and over across that was all the

speaker: EG
words: same place- that was prunes and pears over there. But it's all been pulled

speaker: EG
words: out. Pulled the orchard out down there; apple orchard, and they've put it

speaker: EG
words: in cherries, and they growed up and they pulled them out and he's gardening

speaker: EG
words: it again now, since I come here.

speaker: SS
words: Well, it's got to be, compared to up on the ridges and over towards Troy,

speaker: SS
words: it must have been just great for growing fruit back then here.

speaker: EG
words: Well, every farm on the hill used to have an orchard. This Hdtchison, Jim

speaker: EG
words: Hutchison's place up here, he had p forty acre prune orchard up there.

speaker: SS
words: Forty acre!

speaker: EG
words: Uh-huh. Well, it wasn't quite all prunes, he had apples and cherries and

speaker: EG
words: some other stuff in there, but he had a forty acre orchard in there and

speaker: EG
words: they had a prune dryer. And then there was another forty acre orchard over

speaker: EG
words: here on the Potlatch Ridge. I forget who had it. But it's all pulled out

speaker: EG
words: now, there's nothing there.

speaker: DG
words: But the seasons have changed enough that—

speaker: SS
words: How have they changed?

speaker: DG
words: They seem to be colder or something, and back then you could plant garden

speaker: DG
words: in February and down in the canyon we could have garden, you know, let

speaker: DG
words: tuce and onions and all the stuff like that big enough to eat before twentyfive

speaker: DG
words: back at we lived there for a while, we mail garden stuff. Gather

speaker: DG
words: it, you know, one morning, fix it, and put it in the mail and they'd get it

speaker: DG
words: the next day. And we could send stuff up to them because it'd be so much

speaker: DG
words: earlier. But now, it's getting so that they can have it just about as quick

speaker: DG
words: as we can.

speaker: EG
words: Well, there was more timber up there then and that held the snow longer

speaker: EG
words: than now. They've got that cleared up in there and snow goes out lots

speaker: EG
words: quicker now than it did then.

speaker: EG
words: plant

speaker: DG
words: It's not often that you can . anything down here in February any more.

speaker: DG
words: But there were lots of times way back then you planted your early stuff

speaker: DG
words: even in February.

speaker: SS
words: Well how often would you get snow here in the winter in the early days?

speaker: EG
words: Well, now that's— let's see, in 1904 my brother, Will, went East and got

speaker: EG
words: married, in '04 and '05 my brother, Grover stayed down there at his place

speaker: EG
words: and went to school at Spalding. And they played snowball twamorning-iat

speaker: EG
words: recess all winter. That was all the snow they had. There was one winter

speaker: EG
words: they had a deep snow here that a fellow here in Juliaetta claimed they had

speaker: EG
words: five feet of snow. Five feet of snowfall. He got it awful bare, you know

speaker: EG
words: and he'd clean it off every morning and then measure it and then clean it

speaker: EG
words: off. Well, five feet of snowfall is not five feet of packed snow, by a lot.

speaker: EG
words: It'll take anyway ten feet of snow to make five foot of packed snow. And

speaker: EG
words: that's what they used to have up there in the hills. That snow will pack

speaker: EG
words: clear over the top of fence posts so you could get out there and drive a

speaker: EG
words: team over the top of the fence. (Chuckles)

speaker: DG
words: We never have the snow down here like—

speaker: EG
words: No, they never had much. But the snow that winter— I was feedin' some hor

speaker: EG
words: ses in Catholic Gulch and it was about thirty inches on the ground. And it

speaker: EG
words: was soft. I'd ride down there and you couldn't trust the snow to get right

speaker: EG
words: out and ride on it, your horse'd liable to break through; just too soft,

speaker: EG
words: you had to take it easy.

speaker: EG
words: And the railroad here; we say the wagon road crossed the railroad

speaker: EG
words: eight times between here and Spalding, but it was really the other way.

speaker: EG
words: The road was there first. Down here where Bill Cartan lives it crossed

speaker: EG
words: twice there, went over next to the river, crossed the tracks next to the

speaker: EG
words: river and then come back down further down there, and then down there

speaker: EG
words: where that old schoolhouse is, it crossed up above that and then come out—

speaker: EG
words: well, it come out first down there about where Johnson's barn is down there.

speaker: EG
words: But the river washed it out and they'd change it back up and there was a

speaker: EG
words: little rock cliff down there, oh, it wasn't fifty feet through, but it run

speaker: EG
words: out there fifty or seventy-five feet; just that little narrow ridge of rock

speaker: EG
words: well, that's why the railroad is over where it is, because that little

speaker: EG
words: rock was there and in order to not blast that out, they just passed it up.

speaker: EG
words: Well, then the road got cut out so much, so far back, til they finally got

speaker: EG
words: in and the highway outfits cut that cliff off, and put the road up the

speaker: EG
words: other side of the railroad.

speaker: SS
words: Did your folks; your father get involved there at all in that Arrow Junction

speaker: SS
words: at the depot or anything or was that just you that did that? I understand

speaker: SS
words: that you were- became a depot

speaker: DG
words: No he wasn't.

speaker: EG
words: I worked on the railroad down there for twenty years.

speaker: SS
words: When did you start working on the railroad? About when?

speaker: EG
words: About '38.

speaker: DG
words: He was twenty years on there.

speaker: SS
words: You were already grown by then.

speaker: EG
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: DG
words: Had all his family.

speaker: EG
words: Yes, I was old enough that it was kind of a joke the way I got on there.

speaker: EG
words: They didn't want a man forty-five years old; they wanted him to get laid

speaker: EG
words: off or get rid of him some way. But that was right along about wartime,

speaker: EG
words: and they was a fella— well, no, the first thing they made a relay and they

speaker: EG
words: wanted some men there for about ten days. Then when that was done there

speaker: EG
words: was a man got sick and I stayed on and worked til he come back and then

speaker: EG
words: there was another one went on a vacation and I worked his time while he

speaker: EG
words: was gone and then I thought I was done. I was off for about ten days and

speaker: EG
words: one of the other men fell over on the job and he died before they got him

speaker: EG
words: to the hospital. So I went back and worked til they cut the crew in the

speaker: EG
words: fall, and you're supposed to have a hundred and sixty-three days to make

speaker: EG
words: rights, and I had a hundred and fifty-three when I got laid off. And a

speaker: EG
words: car load of lumber, I think it was, jumped the track up there below Peck,

speaker: EG
words: and run about three quarters of a mile doun on the ties, just cut the road

speaker: EG
words: all to pieces. So they got all the men that they could to go and straight

speaker: EG
words: en that track up and I got on that, and when they got that done I had a

speaker: EG
words: hundred and seventy-three days! (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: So that means you were in.

speaker: EG
words: I had rights.

speaker: DG
words: He just barely made it though.

speaker: EG
words: When they increased the force why, I had to be called.

speaker: SS
words: You had to make it before a certain time?

speaker: EG
words: You had to make that in a year. Had to work a hundred and sixty-three days

speaker: EG
words: a year.

speaker: DG
words: Yeah, and then you had to make it before you was forty-five or in the year

speaker: DG
words: you were forty-five.

speaker: EG
words: Well, any time you made that, when they work you on the railroad, any time

speaker: EG
words: you made that, why, you had your rights, but they didn't want a man that

speaker: EG
words: old, for some reason or other they'd have him laid off or something so

speaker: EG
words: he wouldn't get on. But I just got through that. No, I worked with a young

speaker: EG
words: fellow after that: he was a good young man and they took him on during tie

speaker: EG
words: season, that's when they put on a lot of men, you know, when they're putting

speaker: EG
words: in ties, and he worked for five years during tie season and then was laid

speaker: EG
words: off before he made rights. Then when the war come on.wages went to raising

speaker: EG
words: every place else, but the railroad wouldn't raise wages. So the good young

speaker: EG
words: men, they went for something else, see. They just jumped out of that and

speaker: EG
words: went for something else. The railroad company finally said,no„ they wouldn't

speaker: EG
words: give 'em anymore per hour- psr day- but they'd work ten hours and give 'em

speaker: EG
words: eleven hours pay. You know, that hurt me. I know that the men on there

speaker: EG
words: would have done more work in eight hours than they did in the ten if they'd

speaker: EG
words: a just raised their wages. But the company didn't do that. Then you had

speaker: EG
words: to go on—

speaker: DG
words: When he retired, he had worked by people quitting and changing around and

speaker: DG
words: dying and everything and he'd worked up from way down at the bottom til he

speaker: DG
words: was first man.

speaker: EG
